chapters.title: — Lueger and the Collapse of the Rational World
— Three Responses to the Same Crisis
— The Father's Death and the Self-Analysis
— The Book Nobody Bought / The Evidence Trail
— Anna O. and the Founding Myth
— What Freud Was Before He Was Freud / The Charcot Embarrassment
— The Wednesday Society and the Movement That Built Itself Slowly

follow_up_links.reason: The foundational scholarly work arguing that Vienna's political crisis—the collapse of liberalism—drove intellectuals inward, creating modernism. Schorske frames Freud's psychology as a 'multi-faceted revolt against his father and the custodians of the academy.' Essential for understanding psychoanalysis as a product of political disillusionment, not just scientific inquiry.
follow_up_links.title: Fin-de-Siècle Vienna: Politics and Culture

follow_up_links.reason: The proto-psychoanalytic text featuring the Anna O. case (Bertha Pappenheim). Demonstrates the transition from hypnosis to the 'talking cure' and the break between Breuer and Freud over the sexual etiology of neurosis. The founding document that shows psychoanalysis emerging from clinical failure and disagreement.
follow_up_links.title: Studies on Hysteria

follow_up_links.reason: Freud's masterwork, which he later described as 'a portion of my own self-analysis, my reaction to my father's death.' Published in an edition of 600 copies that didn't sell out for eight years. The book that launched psychoanalysis was initially a commercial failure—a key tension for the episode.
follow_up_links.title: The Interpretation of Dreams

follow_up_links.reason: Documents how Lueger's election as Vienna's antisemitic mayor in 1897 marked the triumph of populist politics over liberalism. Provides essential context for understanding the hostile environment in which Freud worked and why his professorship was delayed.
follow_up_links.title: Karl Lueger: Mayor of Fin de Siècle Vienna

follow_up_links.reason: Ellenberger's critical review of the Anna O. case revealed that Pappenheim was not 'cured' as Breuer claimed—she required further hospitalization. This complicates the founding myth of psychoanalysis and raises questions about the reliability of early case histories.
follow_up_links.title: The Discovery of the Unconscious: The History and Evolution of Dynamic Psychiatry

follow_up_links.reason: The most rigorous philosophical critique of psychoanalysis's scientific status. Grünbaum argues that Freud's clinical evidence is contaminated by suggestion. Essential for the 'complication' section—the ongoing debate about whether psychoanalysis is science or hermeneutics.
follow_up_links.title: The Foundations of Psychoanalysis: A Philosophical Critique

follow_up_links.reason: The museum at Berggasse 19 deliberately preserves the empty space where Freud's couch once stood (now in London) as a reference to his forced exile. The museum's approach—refusing to recreate a 'World of Yesterday'—offers a model for how to tell this story without nostalgia.
follow_up_links.title: Sigmund Freud Museum Vienna (Official Site)

follow_up_links.reason: Documents Vienna's complicated relationship with Freud's legacy—the city that expelled him now claims him as 'Austria's most frequently cited researcher.' Useful for the reflective ending about how we remember difficult histories.
follow_up_links.title: Remembering Freud in Vienna: From Exile to Pop Icon

key_claims.claim: The Interpretation of Dreams was published in an edition of 600 copies that did not sell out for eight years, despite Freud later calling it 'the most valuable of all the discoveries it has been my good fortune to make.'

Freud's father Jacob died on October 23, 1896, triggering a period of self-analysis that Freud later described as his reaction to 'the most important event, the most poignant loss, of a man's life.' This self-analysis directly produced The Interpretation of Dreams.

Karl Lueger was elected Vienna's mayor five times before Emperor Franz Joseph finally confirmed him in 1897, making him the first mayor in Western Europe elected on an explicitly antisemitic platform.

The Vienna Secession was founded on April 3, 1897—the same year Lueger became mayor—with Gustav Klimt as its first president. Both movements represented breaks from the liberal establishment, but in opposite directions.

Freud was awarded the title of associate professor (professor extraordinarius) only in February 1902, after years of delay. The title carried prestige but no salary or teaching duties. He received a full professorship only in 1919.

The Wednesday Psychological Society began meeting at Berggasse 19 in autumn 1902 with just five members: Freud, Wilhelm Stekel, Alfred Adler, Max Kahane, and Rudolf Reitler. By 1906 it had grown to 17 members and was renamed the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society in 1908.

Bertha Pappenheim ('Anna O.'), the patient whose case launched psychoanalysis, was not cured by Breuer's treatment—she required further hospitalization. She later became a prominent Jewish feminist and social worker who founded the League of Jewish Women in 1904 and was skeptical of psychoanalysis.

Freud lived and worked at Berggasse 19 for 47 years (1891-1938) until forced to flee after the Nazi annexation. The Vienna Psychoanalytic Society was dissolved one day after the Anschluss; all but two of its 68 members fled abroad.

When Freud returned from studying with Charcot in Paris and lectured on male hysteria in Vienna, he was met with ridicule. The Viennese medical establishment still held that hysteria was caused by a 'wandering womb' and thus impossible in men.

Karl Popper's 1934 critique identified psychoanalysis as unfalsifiable pseudoscience, a characterization that shaped academic psychology's rejection of Freud for decades. However, recent scholarship argues Popper misunderstood Freud's methodology.
